coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of efficient broad-spectrum UV-absorbing carbon dots as UV absorbers using natural rutin as raw material and their multifunctional applications under acid-base amphoteric conditions
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Yulin
Xu, Zhiqiang
Chen, Congcong
Ye, Weihao
Guo, Baoyan
Qiu, Jiemin
Zhuang, Jianle
Hu, Chaofan
Lei, Bingfu
Hu, Guangqi
Liu, Yingliang
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a kind of novel fully UV absorbing carbon dots (R-CDs) were prepared as UV absorbers by a one-step hydrothermal method using natural renewable rutin as raw material. R-CDs can achieve multifunctional applications under acid-base amphoteric conditions. Under acidic conditions, R-CDs have high absorption efficiency (over 98%) in the whole ultraviolet region (200–400 nm) which have wider UV absorption range than commercial water-soluble organic UV absorbers (BP-4) and are almost completely transmitted in the visible light region. The UV absorption mechanism of R-CDs be investigated to the UV absorption systems of degradable cellulose coating (CMC) and the R-CDs@CMC coating was applied to prevent UV leakage of the 395UV chip. Under alkaline conditions, R-CDs remain fully UV absorbing and good absorption stability, but BP-4 loses its function as a UV absorber. Finally, R-CDs was applied to the UV absorption and anti-blue light systems of PU. The R-CDs@PU are more effective than commercially available anti-blue light lenses. At present, the preparation process of R-CDs is simple and rutin is a natural renewable energy source. So, R-CDs have the potential to become a new generation of water-soluble UV absorbers and be applied to prevent blue light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Natural renewable energy source
Carbon dots
UV full absorption
UV absorbers
Anti-blue light

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV absorption stability of R-CDs and water-soluble organic UV absorber BP-4 under different pH value conditions
The demonstration of the UV shielding effect of R-CDs@CMC and BP-4@CMC
The role of R-CDs in UV-absorption systems of CMC
Application of R-CDs@CMC coating on UV leakage prevention in 395UV LED chips
The application of R-CDs@PU in anti-blue light

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciate the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 12174119 ) and the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province ( 2019A1515110921 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) TEM and HRTEM images of R-CDs. (c) Size distribution of R-CDs. (d) Raman spectra of R-CDs. (e and f) XRD and FTIR spectra of R-CDs. (g)-(i) XPS survey, C 1 s and O 1 s spectra.

(a) UV absorption spectra of R-CDs and Photograph of rutin was irradiated by a UV flashlight with a wavelength of 365 nm (right inset) and Photograph of R-CDs was irradiated by a UV flashlight with a wavelength of 365 nm (left inset). (b) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs under different excitations and Photoluminescence excitation spectra of R-CDs under 340 nm excitations. (c) The UV absorption mechanism of R-CDs (left) and infrared thermal image of pure PU and R-CDs@PU under ultraviolet radiation (right).

(a) UV absorption spectra of R-CDs under different pH conditions. (b) The changes in the positions of the three absorption peaks of R-CDs during the acid-base cycle. (c) UV absorption spectra of BP-4 under different pH conditions. (d) The changes in the positions of the two absorption peaks of BP-4 during the acid-base cycle. (e) Normalized fluorescence spectra of R-CDs during the acid-base cycle. (f) The changes in the positions of the fluorescence peaks of R-CDs during the acid-base cycle. (g) The FT-IR spectra of R-CDs under different pH conditions. (h) The FT-IR spectra of BP-4 under different pH conditions.

(a) UV–Vis absorption-transmission spectra of R-CDs and BP-4. (b) Photos of R-CDs@CMC and BP-4@CMC. (c) UV–Vis absorption-transmission spectra of R-CDs@CMC and pure CMC (d) UV–Vis absorption-transmission spectra of R-CDs@CMC with different layer numbers.

(a) Photos of R-CDs@CMC and BP-4@CMC doped with different R-CDs and BP-4 concentrations. (b) Photos of Rhodamine B shielded by R-CDs@CMC and BP-4@CMC with different concentrations. (c) UV–Vis transmission spectra of R-CDs@CMC doped with different R-CDs concentrations. (d) UV–Vis transmission spectra of BP-4@CMC doped with different BP-4 concentrations. (e) The UV absorption efficiencies of R-CDs@CMC and BP-4@CMC. (f) The absorption efficiencies of R-CDs@CMC and BP-4@CMC at UVA and UVB bands.

(a) The schematic diagram of 395UV chip packaging. (b) Spectrogram of lamp beads covered with lampshades coated with different R-CDS@PU coatings. (c) The color coordinates of the lamp beads covered with lampshades coated with different R-CDS@PU coatings.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of R-CDs under acidic and basic conditions. (b) UV–Vis transmission spectra of R-CDs@PU and commercially available anti-blue light lenses, respectively. (c) the integrated areas of commercially available anti-blue light lenses and R-CDs@PU coatings in the wavelength of 400–470 nm, respectively. (d) and (e) The absorption efficiencies of commercially available anti-blue light lenses and R-CDs@PU coatings for the different wavelengths of light were calculated, respectively. (f) The tensile mechanical properties of the pure PU and R-CDs@PU.
The UV absorption efficiency of R-CDs@CMC doped with different concentrations of R-CDs in UVA and UVB.
The UV absorption efficiency of BP-4@CMC doped with different concentrations of BP-4 in UVA and UVB.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of efficient broad-spectrum UV-absorbing carbon dots as UV absorbers using natural rutin as raw material and their multifunctional applications under acid-base amphoteric conditions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biomass Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biomass Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aKey Laboratory for Biomass Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University, Guangzhou 510665, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCollege of Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University, Guangzhou 510665, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a kind of novel fully UV absorbing carbon dots (R-CDs) were prepared as UV absorbers by a one-step hydrothermal method using natural renewable rutin as raw material. R-CDs can achieve multifunctional applications under acid-base amphoteric conditions. Under acidic conditions, R-CDs have high absorption efficiency (over 98%) in the whole ultraviolet region (200–400 nm) which have wider UV absorption range than commercial water-soluble organic UV absorbers (BP-4) and are almost completely transmitted in the visible light region. The UV absorption mechanism of R-CDs be investigated to the UV absorption systems of degradable cellulose coating (CMC) and the R-CDs@CMC coating was applied to prevent UV leakage of the 395UV chip. Under alkaline conditions, R-CDs remain fully UV absorbing and good absorption stability, but BP-4 loses its function as a UV absorber. Finally, R-CDs was applied to the UV absorption and anti-blue light systems of PU. The R-CDs@PU are more effective than commercially available anti-blue light lenses. At present, the preparation process of R-CDs is simple and rutin is a natural renewable energy source. So, R-CDs have the potential to become a new generation of water-soluble UV absorbers and be applied to prevent blue light.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As we all know, ultraviolet light is one of the natural components of sunlight [1]. According to the different wavelengths, UV light can be divided into three parts: UVC (200–280 nm), UVB (280–320 nm) and UVA (320–400 nm) [2]. Among them, UVC will be absorbed by the ozone layer, and only UVB and UVA can break through the ozone layer to reach the Earth's surface [3]. UVB is more destructive to polymer materials, which will lead to the generation of free radicals in polymer [4], thus destroying the internal binding energy of the polymer, gradually decomposing polymer materials, and lowering its mechanical properties [5]. As for UVA, although the sensitive wavelengths of most polymers are not in the UVA range, UVA is very harmful to the human body. UVA can induce skin tanning [6], eye damage [7], prolong-term exposure can cause skin damage, make the skin lose elasticity, appear wrinkles and induce phototoxic and photoallergic reactions [8]. Furthermore, this part of the ultraviolet light will also lead to the decomposition and fading of dyes [9].

There are two kinds of commercially available UV absorbers, organic UV absorbers and inorganic UV absorbers [10]. Organic UV absorbers (mainly include salicylic acid, benzophenone, benzotriazole and triazine, etc.) which is commonality that contain conjugated π-π* electronic systems. Most of them are oil-soluble rather than water-soluble, and there are only two commercially available types that can be dissolved in water, 2‑hydroxy-4‑methoxy benzophenone-5-sulfonic acid (BP-4) and 2-phenylbenzimi-dazole-5-sulfonic acid (UV-T) [11]. Organic UV absorber accompanied by many disadvantages, such as narrow absorption range, toxicity and expensiveness, etc. [12]. Inorganic UV absorbers (mainly include zinc oxide, titanium dioxide, etc.) generally has low absorption efficiency, poor permeability, low light transmittance, poor dispersibility and high cost, etc. [13,14]. Moreover, this type of UV shielding agent has poor affinity with organic materials, and it is difficult to blend into the polymer materials [15].

Rutin, also known as rutin and vitamin P, is a natural renewable energy source, with the molecular formula of C27H30O16, a kind of natural flavonoid glycoside [16], belonging to a group of flavonoid ligands widely existing in plants. Rutin is a natural renewable energy source, it is cheap and simple to extract, and can be obtained from nature in a constant stream. Accordingly, it has good prospects as a raw material for hydrothermal carbonization [17].

Sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) is a water-soluble polymer. When it is dissolved in water, a large number of intermolecular hydrogen bonds can be formed [18]. The surface of R-CDs also has many hydroxyl groups. Therefore, R-CDs can be dispersed in an aqueous solution of carboxymethyl cellulose and the composite coating can be obtained by casting. Since cellulose is biodegradable and the R-CDs raw material is also renewable biomass, the composite coating is very green and environmentally friendly. At present, no one has used full UV absorbing carbon dots as UV absorbers for the UV absorption of cellulose coatings.

CDs are sphere-like nanoparticles with particle size less than 10 nm [19], which have size limiting effect and photoluminescence properties [20]. They can emit visible light under UV excitation, so they have UV absorption properties [21]. As far as the available published literature is concerned, no one has yet synthesized UV fully absorbing carbon dots as UV absorbers using a single molecule as raw material. And research on the synthesis of carbon dots using natural renewable biomass as raw material and using them as ultraviolet absorption is very rare. At present, only Li et al. have prepared N-doping CDs from waste acorn cups and explore an UV absorber in the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film [22]. However, these CDs can't block full UV bands as the UV absorber and the transmission of visible light is low. Xie et al. prepared silane-doping GQDs (SiGQDs) which have full UV bonds blocking and emit visible luminescent, but it is very inefficient at UV blocking [23]. Samuel C. Hess et al. get a transparent UV protection film which is synthesized by CQDs in an aqueous polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) solution. However, the visible bonds transparency of the films is only 80% and they can only absorb UVA bands [9]. In this study, R-CDs were prepared from natural renewable rutin and it not only absorbs all UV light but also doesn't affect the transmission of visible light. Compared with the commercial water-soluble organic UV absorber BP-4, R-CDs have a wider UV absorption range. After subjecting R-CDs and BP-4 to different pH values, it was found that R-CDs remain fully UV absorbing at different pH conditions and maintain good absorption stability, but BP-4 loses its function as a UV absorber under alkaline conditions. The UV absorption mechanism of R-CDs has also been investigated and applied to the UV absorption systems of CMC and the anti-blue light system of PU. At present, the preparation process of R-CDs is simple, environmentally friendly and rutin is a natural renewable energy source [24], it has the potential to become a new generation of water-soluble UV absorbers and can also be applied to prevent blue light.

Rutin (AR) and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Aladdin Industries, Inc. 2‑hydroxy-4‑methoxy benzophenone-5-sulfonic acid (BP-4) were purchased from Taobao, Polyurethane (PU) and Sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMCNa) were purchased from YuanYe Industries, Inc. Sodium hydroxide and concentrated sulphuric acid anhydrous purchased from Macklin Industries, Inc. All reagents without further purification. Ultrapure water was used in the whole experiment.

A new type of all-ultraviolet absorbing carbon dots (R-CDs) was synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method with natural renewable rutin as raw material. Rutin (0.16 g) and ethanol (20 mL) were sealed in a steel autoclave with a PTFE liner and placed in an oven for 6 h at 180 °C. Waiting for the solution to cool to 25 °C, and transformed from colorless into yellow, indicating the successful synthesis of R-CDs. Then, the reaction solution was filtered through an aqueous needle filter with a pore size of 0.22 um and a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 500 was used to dialysis R-CDs for 36 h Finally, the purified product was suspended in oven at 80 °C for 72 h to obtain a yellow solid CDs and calculate the yield.

UV–vis absorption-transmission spectra of R-CDs were measured with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (U-3900, Hitachi). The XRD profiles of R-CDs were measured with a Persee XD-2X/M4600. The Elemental percentages of R-CDs and XPS images were detected using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratos) The FT-IR spectra of R-CDs were measured with a Nicolet Avatar 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer. TEM images and HRTEM images of R-CDs were obtained using a JEOL2010 electron microscope. Infrared thermography photographs of the slides were recorded with FLIR. The fluorescence spectral profiles of R-CDs were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi). The Raman spectrum was recorded by Alpha300R (Germany, Wetzlar). The UV lamp used in the whole experiment is 365 nm and 85 W produced by Zoomlion UV Optical Factory.

Under continuous stirring condition, take 10 mL of commercial aqueous coating (PU) and 0.3 g R-CDs in a beaker. Then, continue to stir until they are mixed well and adjust the pH value of the mixture to 10. Finally, apply them to the slide glass and dry them in an oven at 60 °C to get a R-CDs@PU sample, and stored the fabricated R-CDs@PU in dry containers.

Firstly, 0.5 g of CMC was dispersed in 200 ml of deionised water and stirred for 6 h After the CMC had completely dissolved and formed a clear mucilage, it was evenly divided into 10 parts. Then, R-CDs and BP-4 were weighed (0.2 g, 0.4 g, 0.6 g, 0.8 g) and dispersed in the clear CMC mucilage. The final R-CDs@CMC and BP-4@CMC mucilage were obtained at concentrations of 10 mg/ml, 20 mg/ml, 30 mg/ml and 40 mg/ml, respectively. The different concentrations of R-CDs@CMC and BP-4@CMC mucilage were applied on transparent slides and then dried in an oven at 40 °C to obtain C-1, C-2, C-3 and C-4 R-CDs@CMC coatings and B-1, B-2, B-3 and B-4 BP-4@CMC coatings.

The synthesized R-CDs were first characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM photographs in Fig. 1 a confirmed that R-CDs has excellent dispersion in aqueous solution with an average particle size about 2.9 nm Fig. 1c. The HRTEM images in Fig. 1b showed a 0.21 nm lattice stripes, corresponding to the (100) face of graphite [25]. The Raman spectrum of rutin and R-CDs are given in Fig. 1d. And there is a significant difference in the Raman spectrum between rutin and R-CDs. The Raman spectrum of R-CDs shows two broad characteristic absorption bands at 1380 cm−1 (D-band) and 1574 cm−1 (G-band) respectively, with the characteristic absorption band at 1380 cm−1 (D band) indicates the existence of a CC bond of sp3 character and the characteristic absorption band at 1574 cm−1 (G-band) indicating the existence of C = C of sp2 character, where ID/IG = 0.77, indicating a high degree of carbonization [26]. The XRD patterns of rutin and R-CDs are given in Fig. 1e. There is a significant difference in the XRD patterns between rutin and R-CDs, with the characteristic diffraction peak of R-CDs at 2θ=22.4° and 2θ=42.4°, corresponding to the graphite (100) plane. However, rutin has three sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ=10.7°, 15.6° and 26.4°, respectively. This result indicates that the original crystalline form of rutin was destroyed and graphitization increased after high temperature carbonization, which is consistent with the Raman and TEM results.

The chemical composition and surface functional groups of the synthesized R-CDs were further investigated by FT-IR and XPS.As shown in Fig. 1f, there are also significant differences in FT-IR patterns between rutin and R-CDs. The surface functional group of R-CDs is simplex that a broad absorption band at 3430 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of -OH, the absorption peak at 1650 cm−1 and 1480 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C = O and the aromatic ring C = C, respectively. However, the FT-IR spectrum of rutin shows a broad absorption band at 3430 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of -OH [27], the absorption peak at 1650 cm−1 and 1480 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C = O and the aromatic ring C = C [28], respectively, shows that all of the functional groups were carried by rutin. The XPS full-scan spectra of the R-CDs and the high-resolution spectra of C1s and O1s are listed in Fig. 1(g-i). The XPS full-scan spectra show that R-CDs are mainly composed of C and O elements with binding energies of 284.49 eV and 530.17 eV, corresponding to C1s and O1s, respectively in Fig. 1g [29]. The percentages of C and O atoms are 71.94% and 28.06%, respectively. The C1s high-resolution spectrum in Fig. 1h shows the presence of C = C (284.8 eV), CO (286.6 eV) and C = O (287.6 eV) on the surface of R-CDs [30]. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s can be fitted with two peaks at 531.2 eV and 533.1 eV in Fig. 1i, which are attributed to C = O and C = C, respectively [31]. The results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy are consistent with the FT-IR results, which indicate that R-CDs contain -OH, aromatic rings and C = O functional groups on the surface.

Fig. 2 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of R-CDs. There are three absorption peaks of R-CDs, which are located at 255 nm, 298 nm and 368 nm respectively. The absorption peak at 255 nm is attributed to the π-π* leap of the benzene ring C = C bond attached to the surface of the carbon core [32]. While the absorption peak at 298 nm is attributed to the π-π* leap of the aromatic cluster attached to the surface of the carbon core, and the absorption peak at 368 nm is attributed to the n-π* leap of the C = O bond [33]. We can prove this from the simple infrared spectrum and the XPS spectra of R-CDs in Fig. 1f.

Because of its size-limiting effect and photoluminescence properties, CDs can emit visible light under UV excitation (inset of Fig. 2a). In Fig. 2b, when R-CDs is excited by laser with wavelengths of 320–380 nm, it exhibits typical excitation-independent behavior [34]. When R-CDs is excited by UV light at 340 nm, it has the strongest blue fluorescence emission at 450 nm. However, its PLQY is only 6.9%, and most of the energy absorbed by R-CDs is dissipated in the form of heat energy. In Fig. 2c, one end of the slide was coated with R-CDs@PU and the middle with pure PU, then placed it under a UV lamp for one minute and took infrared thermography to record the change in temperature of the slide. The temperature coated with R-CDs@PU is 31.6 °C, while that in the place coated with pure PU is only 29.0 °C, demonstrating that most of the energy absorbed by the carbon dots is dispersed in the form of thermal energy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV absorption stability of R-CDs and water-soluble organic UV absorber BP-4 under different pH value conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The abundant functional groups on R-CDs surface cause its absorption and fluorescence efficiency to be considerably affected by pH value [35]. The ultimate purpose of R-CDs is to act as water-soluble UV absorbers and to apply them in water-soluble coatings. However, the pH of different water-soluble coatings varies. Therefore, the stability of R-CDs and BP-4 was investigated under different pH conditions.

Fig. 3 a and 3c show the UV–Vis absorption spectra of R-CDs and BP-4 at different pH conditions, respectively. It can be seen that R-CDs and BP-4 have two states of absorption in the acid-base amphoteric state. The reason for this phenomenon is the effect of deprotonation on R-CDs and BP-4 rather than a change in the structure or surface functional groups of R-CDs and BP-4 under alkaline conditions. This was demonstrated by utilizing the reversibility of the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of R-CDs and BP-4 during the acid-base cycle and the invariance of the IR spectra under different pH value conditions. First, the pH value of the R-CDs and BP-4 solutions were adjusted to alkaline, and then their UV–Vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra and FT-IR spectra were measured; then the alkaline R-CDs and BP-4 solutions were adjusted to acid, and then their UV–Vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra and FT-IR spectra were measured, and three sets of cycles were made in this manner. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of R-CDs and BP-4 solutions during the acid-base cycle were recorded in Fig. S1 and S2, respectively, and the fluorescence spectra of R-CDs were recorded in Fig. S3. To demonstrate more visually the changes in the absorption peaks and fluorescence emission peaks of R-CDs and BP-4 during acid-base cycling, the absorption peaks of R-CDs and BP-4 were recorded in Fig. 3b and 3d, and the normalized fluorescence spectra and fluorescence emission peaks of R-CDs were recorded in Fig. 3e and 3f, respectively. It can be seen that the absorption and fluorescence emission peaks of R-CDs return to their original positions after each set of acid-base cycling, and they are reversible, indicating that the structure of R-CDs does not change during the acid-base cycling process. The FT-IR spectra of R-CDs and BP-4 solutions during acid-base cycling were recorded in Fig. 3g and 3h, respectively, and it was found that the FT-IR spectra of R-CDs and BP-4 did not change during acid-base cycling, indicating that the surface functional groups of R-CDs did not change during acid-base cycling. It indicates that the difference in UV absorption of R-CDs under acid and alkaline conditions is due to the influence of hydrogen protons rather than changes in the structure or surface functional groups of R-CDs, which are all stable under acid and alkaline conditions.

It is not surprising to find that R-CDs can still achieve full UV absorption under alkaline conditions, while BP-4 loses its value as a UV absorber under alkaline conditions. For this reason, R-CDs can be used as UV absorbers under acidic conditions and as an anti-blue light coating under alkaline conditions, due to the difference in the range of UV absorption of R-CDs under acidic and alkaline conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The demonstration of the UV shielding effect of R-CDs@CMC and BP-4@CMC
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a shows the UV–Vis absorption-transmission spectra of R-CDs and BP-4. R-CDs can absorb all the UV light and the transmittance in the visible region is also above 95%. However, BP-4 can only absorb the UV light in the region of 200–340 nm, it can't absorb the UV light in the region of 340–400 nm.

As shown in Fig. 4b, the R-CDs and BP-4 are well dispersed in the CMC matrix without obvious aggregation. The addition of R-CDs allows for an additional absorption peak at 362 nm compared to pure CMC, giving CMC full UV absorption capability (Fig. 4c).

In addition, full UV absorption can be achieved by increasing the number of layers of the R-CDs@CMC As shown in Fig. 4d, when the concentration of R-CDs is 10 mg/mL, the full UV absorption can't be achieved with a single layer of R-CDs@CMC, but 100% UV absorption can be achieved with a three-layer R-CDs@CMC, and the transmittance in the visible region is over 95%. Therefore, 100% UV absorption can be achieved by either increasing the concentration of R-CDs or increasing the number of layers of the film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of R-CDs in UV-absorption systems of CMC
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 a, the R-CDs@CMC are all highly transparent, and the color of the R-CDs@ CMC becomes darker as the concentration of doped R-CDs increases, but this doesn't affect the high transparency of the films.

Here, we use an organic dye (rhodamine B) to better demonstrate R-CDs@CMC full UV absorption effect (Fig. 5b), in which the aqueous solution of rhodamine B is orange-yellow under 365 ultraviolet light and pink under visible light. When the aqueous solution of rhodamine B is shielded by R-CDs@CMC doped with different concentrations of R-CDs, it exhibits a clear regulation of colour change. The colour of Rhodamine B progressively changes from orange to the original pink as the R-CDs concentration increases. When the colour of rhodamine B changes to pink, it signifies that the R-CDs@CMC has absorbed all the UV light (Fig. 5b). But when the aqueous solution of rhodamine B is shielded by BP-4@CMC doped with different concentrations of BP-4, its colour is orange, indicating that BP-4@CMC does not absorb 365 UV light (Fig. 5b).

Fig. 5c shows the UV–Vis transmission properties of R-CDs@CMC doped with different R-CDs concentrations, and the UV absorption intensity of R-CDs@CMC increases with the increase of R-CDs concentration. When the concentration of R-CDs increases to 40 mg/mL, the R-CDs@CMC can achieve 99.9% UV absorption efficiency and the transmittance in the visible region is also above 95%. Fig. 5d shows the UV–Vis transmission properties of BP-4@CMC doped with different BP-4 concentrations, and the UV absorption intensity of BP-4@CMC increases with the increase of BP-4 concentration, too. The UV absorption efficiencies of R-CDs@CMC and BP-4@CMC are summarized in Fig. 5e. When the concentration of BP-4 increases to 40 mg/mL, the BP-4@CMC can just achieve 77.6% UV absorption efficiency, while that of R-CDs@CMC can reach 99.9%.

We found that both R-CDs@CMC doped with different concentrations of R-CDs and BP-4@CMC doped with different concentrations of BP-4 could achieve 100% absorption in the UVB band, the main difference between them being in the UVA band.

The R-CDs@CMC and BP-4@CMC UV absorption efficiency in UVA and UVB can be calculated by the following formula [36,37]:

In the formula, T λ is the transmission at λ. T
R − CDs@CMCinUVA and T
BP − 4@CMCinUVA are the average transmittances of R-CDs@ CMC and BP-4@ CMC in the wavelength region of (320–400 nm), respectively A
R − CDs@CMCinUVA and A
BP − 4@CMCinUVA are the absorption efficiency of R-CDs@CMC and BP-4@CMC, respectively.

Based on Eqs. (1) and (3), the UV absorption efficiency of R-CDs@CMC doped with different concentrations of R-CDs at UVA and UVB are shown in Fig. S4 and Table 1 . Based on Eqs. (2) and (4), the UV absorption efficiency of BP-4@CMC doped with different concentrations of BP-4 at UVA and UVB are shown in Fig. S5 and Table 2 . The absorption efficiencies of R-CDs@CMC and BP-4@CMC in UVA and UVB bands are summarized in Fig. 5f. When the concentration of BP-4 is 40 mg/mL, the UV absorption efficiency of BP-4@CMC in the UVA band is only 66.4%, while that of R-CDs@CMC can reach 99.9%.

R-CDs were successfully applied in UV absorption systems of CMC. The addition of R-CDs not only does not affect the original properties of the CMC, but also endows it with full UV absorption properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of R-CDs@CMC coating on UV leakage prevention in 395UV LED chips
ce:section.ce:para: It can be seen from Fig. 5c that when the concentration of R-CDs is 40 mg/mL, the R-CDs@CMC coating can absorb all the ultraviolet rays and the transmittance in the visible light region is 96%. Therefore, it can be applied to UV LED chips to prevent UV leakage. Because R-CDs@CMC coating can absorb ultraviolet light that is harmful to human body without affecting the visible light region.

Fig. 6 a shows a schematic diagram of UV chip packaging. Firstly, the blue, green, and red primary color phosphors are mixed in AB glue in a certain proportion, and then dot-mapped on the 395UV chip. Subsequently, the R-CDs@CMC coating doped with different concentrations of R-CDs was coated on the inner surface of the lampshade, and the lampshade was installed after the R-CDs@CMC coating has dried.

Finally, the spectrogram of the encapsulated lamp beads was tested with a spectral color illuminance meter (Fig. 6b). It can be seen that as the concentration of R-CDs increases, the emission peak of the UV chip becomes lower and lower. When the concentration of R-CDs was 40 mg/mL, the emission peak of the UV chip almost disappeared, and the R-CDs@CMC coating did not affect the spectrum in the visible region. This result is consistent with the results of the UV–Vis transmission spectra of the R-CDs@CMC coating in Fig. 6c.

The results measured under different lamp shades are displayed on the CIE coordinate system (Fig. 6c) and it can be seen that the color coordinates of the lamp beads change from (0.3689,0.3964) to (0.3836,0.4049) as the concentration of R-CDs increases. This is attributable to the lowering of the emission peak of the UV chip as the concentration of R-CDs increases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of R-CDs@PU in anti-blue light
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of R-CDs under acidic and basic conditions. Under alkaline conditions, the UV absorption peaks of R-CDs were red-shifted by 39 nm due to deprotonation. Therefore, an anti-blue light R-CDs@PU coating has been produced using this property. Fig. 7b shows the UV–Visible transmission spectra of R-CDs@PU and commercially available anti-blue light lenses. Commercially available anti-blue light lenses can only shield light up to a wavelength of 410 nm, which is not very effective in the blue light region, and only about 10% for light with a wavelength of 450 nm. On the other hand, the R-CDs@PU coating can effectively shield light up to a wavelength of 450 nm, with a shielding effect of up to 90% for wavelengths up to 450 nm.

The absorption efficiency of R-CDs@PU coatings and commercially available anti-blue light lenses for light in the wavelength region of 400–470 nm (blue light) can be calculated the following formula:
(5)
T
a n t i − b l u e
l i g h t
l e n s e s
=
∫
400
470
T λ
d λ
/
∫

470

(6)
A
a n t i − b l u e

= 1 −
A

(7)

R − C D s @ P U

R − C D s @ P U

In the formula, T λ is the transmission at λ. A
anti − blue light lenses
and A
R − CDs@PU are the average transmittances of commercially available anti-blue light lenses and R-CDs@PU coating in the wavelength region of (400–470 nm), respectively. A
nati − blue light lenses
and A
R − CDs@PU are the absorption efficiency of commercially available anti-blue light lenses and R-CDs@PU coating, respectively.

Fig. 7c show the integrated areas of commercially available anti-blue light lenses and R-CDs@PU coatings in the wavelength of 400–470 nm. Based on Eqs. (5) and (6), it can be calculated that the absorption efficiency of commercially available anti-blue light lenses for light in the wavelength region of (400–470 nm) is 25.9%. Based on Eqs. (7) and (8), it can be calculated that the absorption efficiency of R-CDs@PU coatings for light in the wavelength region of (400–470 nm) is 89.3%. Therefore, the R-CDs@PU coating can be used as an anti-blue light coating.

Divide natural light into three parts: 1. Ultraviolet light (200–400 nm); 2. Blue light (400–470 nm); 3. Other visible light (470–800 nm). The absorption efficiencies of commercially available anti-blue light lenses and R-CDs@PU coatings for the three parts of light were calculated, respectively, as shown in Fig. 7d and 7e, both commercially available anti-blue light lenses and R-CDs@PU coatings can completely block UV light without affecting the transmittance of visible light in the 470–800 nm band. However, the blue light shielding efficiency of commercially available anti-blue light lenses is only 25.9%, which is lower than 89.3% of the R-CDs@PU coating.

The incorporation of R-CDs into the PU film not only gives the PU film anti-blue light properties, but also improves the tensile mechanical properties of the film to a great extent (Fig. 7f). With the increase of the concentration of R-CDs, the tensile mechanical properties of the films become better and better, which probably because the surface of R-CDs has abundant functional groups, such as -OH and C = O. They can form hydrogen bonds with PU, thus making the mechanical properties significantly improved.

In summary, a full wavelength of UV-absorbing R-CDs were prepared as UV absorbers by a one-step hydrothermal method using natural renewable rutin as raw material. By comparing the UV–Vis absorption spectra of rutin and R-CDs and combing the simple infrared spectra of R-CDs, the UV absorption mechanism of R-CDs was obtained. The absorption peak at 255 nm is attributed to the π-π* leap of the benzene ring C = C bond attached to the surface of the carbon core. While the absorption peak at 298 nm is attributed to the π-π* leap of the aromatic cluster attached to the surface of the carbon core, and the absorption peak at 368 nm is attributed to the n-π* leap of the C = O bond. And most of the energy absorbed by R-CDs is dispersed in the form of thermal energy. Since R-CDs have two absorption states in the acid-base amphoteric state, the multifunctional application of R-CDs in the acid-base amphoteric state is realized. Under acidic conditions, R-CDs have high absorption efficiency (over 98%) in the whole ultraviolet region (200–400 nm) and are almost completely transmitted in the visible light region. R-CDs have a wider UV absorption range than BP-4. Then, R-CDs was applied to the UV absorption systems of degradable cellulose coating (CMC). Not only do R-CDs@CMC have full UV absorption capabilities, but both R-CDs and CMC are environmentally friendly. Finally, the R-CDs@CMC coating was applied to prevent UV leakage of the 395UV chip. Under alkaline conditions, R-CDs remain fully UV absorbing and maintain good absorption stability, but BP-4 loses its function as a UV absorber under alkaline conditions. Then, R-CDs was applied to the UV absorption and anti-blue light systems of PU. And the R-CDs@PU are more effective than commercially available anti-blue light lenses. At present, the preparation process of R-CDs is simple, low cost, safe, environmentally friendly and rutin is a natural renewable energy source. So, R-CDs has the potential to become a new generation of water-soluble UV absorbers and can also be applied to prevent blue light.

Yulin Xu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Zhiqiang Xu: Methodology, Data curation. Congcong Chen: Methodology, Data curation. Weihao Ye: Data curation, Formal analysis. Baoyan Guo: Data curation. Jiemin Qiu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Jianle Zhuang: Data curation. Chaofan Hu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Bingfu Lei: Data curation, Formal analysis. Guangqi Hu: Funding acquisition. Yingliang Liu: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (12174119) and the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2019A1515110921).
